questions: Step 7: When useClipDrag captures the pointer (setPointerCapture), does the library still fire onActionMoveStart before capture happens? If so, we may need to keep a minimal onActionMoveStart to prevent the library from applying its own drag — or we need to capture earlier (on pointerdown instead of first move past threshold). This is the key risk in Phase 3.

Step 7: The library's onChange callback is currently used for same-row horizontal moves (useTimelineEditing.ts:353-360). If we remove onActionMove* but keep onChange, does the library still call onChange during drags? Or does the library only call onChange as a result of its own internal move handling (which we'd be suppressing)?

Step 14: Is the height hack in EditorPaneTab actually wrong, or is it a workaround for getEffectivePaneHeights not knowing about the editor pane? The current getEffectivePaneHeights takes idealEditorPaneHeight and generationsPaneHeight but doesn't know that both panes stack from the top — is the issue that these panes share top-of-viewport space?

Should the floating ghost clone (the dragged-clip visual in useClipDrag) also become a React component, or is imperative DOM cloning acceptable here since it's cloning an arbitrary existing DOM element?
success_criteria: Only one indicator system exists — the <DropIndicator> React portal component — used by both internal clip drag and external HTML5 drag-drop
No imperative document.createElement or document.body.appendChild calls for drop indicators remain anywhere in the codebase
actionDragStateRef is internal to useClipDrag, not threaded through context or shared across files
useTimelineEditing.ts no longer exists; its concerns are split into useClipEditing (~250 LOC), useExternalDrop, useAssetManagement, and useClipResize
The @xzdarcy library's onActionMove* callbacks are either removed (if Step 6 confirms they're unnecessary) or clearly documented as required coordination points
EditorPaneTab has no local height calculation — all height logic lives in getEffectivePaneHeights
All existing user interactions work: clip drag (horizontal + cross-track), clip resize, external file/generation/asset drop, split, delete, mute, text add
assumptions: The @xzdarcy/react-timeline-editor library fires onActionMoveStart synchronously on pointerdown before useClipDrag can capture — this needs verification in Step 6 but the plan accounts for both outcomes
The library's resize callbacks (onActionResizeStart/End) are genuinely used and cannot be replaced by our own pointer handling — resize is lower priority and works fine

The floating ghost (DOM clone of the dragged clip) should remain imperative since it clones an arbitrary rendered element — making it declarative would require rendering a separate React component that mirrors the clip
Only one clip can be dragged at a time, so actionDragStateRef can be simplified from Record<string, ActionDragState> to ActionDragState | null

The RAF throttle pattern in onTimelineDragOver is still needed even with React state — dragover fires at 60fps and we don't want 60 React re-renders per second, so the hook should batch into a ref and flush on RAF
getEffectivePaneHeights can be extended to handle the editor-pane-overlaps-generations case without breaking the existing degraded-tier test contract
